5546d808-07fb-4ff5-866c-8d8b0c3c891a.text:
F-Score Driven Max Margin Neural Network for Named Entity Recognition in Chinese Social Media

Hangfeng He and Xu Sun
MOE Key Laboratory of Computational Linguistics, Peking University School of Electronics Engineering and Computer Science, Peking University
{ hangfenghe, xusun } @pku.edu.cn

Abstract

We focus on named entity recognition (NER) for Chinese social media.

a3af1051-c8e9-4740-a019-5b9c9d7b8518.label: text_chunk
a3af1051-c8e9-4740-a019-5b9c9d7b8518.text:
With massive unlabeled text and quite lim- ited labelled corpus, we propose a semi- supervised learning model based on B- LSTM neural network. To take advan- tage of traditional methods in NER such as CRF, we combine transition probabil- ity with deep learning in our model.

 To bridge the gap between label accuracy and F-score of NER, we construct a model which can be directly trained on F-score.

e5a950ba-1026-446a-af57-1779b0072eef.label: text_chunk
e5a950ba-1026-446a-af57-1779b0072eef.text:
When considering the instability of F- score driven method and meaningful in- formation provided by label accuracy, we propose an integrated method to train on both F-score and label accuracy. Our in- tegrated model yields substantial improve- ment over previous state-of-the-art result.

1 Introduction

With the development of Internet, social me- dia plays an important role in information exchange.

63994ca6-1825-466f-90df-32afb9fbeaed.label: text_chunk
63994ca6-1825-466f-90df-32afb9fbeaed.text: The natural language processing tasks on social media are more challenging which draw attention of many researchers (Li and Liu, 2015; Habib and van Keulen, 2015; Radford et al., 2015; Cherry and Guo, 2015). As the foundation of many downstream applications (Weissenborn et al., 2015; Delgado et al.

4edc7734-e3b3-46bd-a94a-bf07dad8bfd9.label: text_chunk
4edc7734-e3b3-46bd-a94a-bf07dad8bfd9.text: , 2014; Hajishirzi et al., 2013) such as information extrac- tion, named entity recognition (NER) deserves more research in prevailing and challenging social media text. NER is a task to identify names in texts and to assign names with particular types (Sun et al., 2009; Sun, 2014; Sun et al., 2014; He and Sun, 2017).

2347e844-94f8-4f2e-afd3-3a13ea662aeb.label: text_chunk
2347e844-94f8-4f2e-afd3-3a13ea662aeb.text:
It is the informality of social media that discourages accuracy of NER
systems. While efforts in English have nar- rowed the gap between social media and formal domains (Cherry and Guo, 2015), the task in Chinese remains challenging. It is caused by Chinese logographic characters which lack many clues to indicate whether a word is a name, such as capitalization.

8904390f-bb38-4e4d-8a22-9c4abe73eedc.label: text_chunk
8904390f-bb38-4e4d-8a22-9c4abe73eedc.text: The scant labelled Chi- nese social media corpus makes the task more challenging (Neelakantan and Collins, 2015; Skeppstedt, 2014; Liu et al., 2015).To address the problem, one approach is to use the lexical embeddings learnt from massive unla- beled text.

0e5526a5-e70e-4dc4-a2d8-3ed70d4f123f.label: text_chunk
0e5526a5-e70e-4dc4-a2d8-3ed70d4f123f.text: To take better advantage of unlabeled text, Peng and Dredze (2015) evaluates three types of embeddings for Chinese text, and shows the effectiveness of positional character embeddings with experiments. Considering the value of word segmentation in Chinese NER, another approach is to construct an integrated model to jointly train learned representations for both predicting word segmentations and NER (Peng and Dredze, 2016).

fc5b837e-e8cd-44c2-9b36-afd4a7af4677.label: text_chunk
fc5b837e-e8cd-44c2-9b36-afd4a7af4677.text:
However, the two above approaches are imple- mented within CRF model.

 We construct a semi- supervised model based on B-LSTM neural net- work to learn from the limited labelled corpus by using lexical information provided by massive un- labeled text. To shrink the gap between label ac- curacy and F-Score, we propose a method to di- rectly train on F-Score rather than label accuracy in our model.

d8b5f17c-9f00-4b9d-b571-20d37273d544.label: text_chunk
d8b5f17c-9f00-4b9d-b571-20d37273d544.text:
In addition, we propose an inte- grated method to train on both F-Score and label accuracy.

 Specifically, we make contributions as follows:

• We propose a method to directly train on F- Score rather than label accuracy.

 In addition, we propose an integrated method to train on both F-Score and label accuracy.

• We combine transition probability into our B- LSTM based max margin neural network to

form structured output in neural network.

0be92320-adbe-441b-95c5-2d8f8f22fcb8.label: text_chunk
0be92320-adbe-441b-95c5-2d8f8f22fcb8.text:
• We evaluate two methods to use lexical em- beddings from unlabeled text in neural net- work.

2 Model

We construct a semi-supervised model which is based on B-LSTM neural network and combine transition probability to form structured output. We propose a method to train directly on F-Score in our model. In addition, we propose an inte- grated method to train on both F-Score and label accuracy.

be84f0db-4d60-487b-a89e-e096354b1f4e.label: text_chunk
be84f0db-4d60-487b-a89e-e096354b1f4e.text:
2.1 Transition Probability

B-LSTM neural network can learn from past input features and LSTM layer makes it more efficient (Hammerton, 2003; Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997; Chen et al., 2015; Graves et al., 2006). How- ever, B-LSTM cannot learn sentence level label information. Huang et al. (2015) combine CRF to use sentence level label information.

fc15a6ae-f43c-4109-bdfa-3d270928b98c.label: text_chunk
fc15a6ae-f43c-4109-bdfa-3d270928b98c.text:
We combine transition probability into our model to gain sentence level label information. To combine transition probability into B-LSTM neural network, we construct a Max Margin Neural Network (MMNN) (Pei et al., 2014) based on B-LSTM. The prediction of label in position t is given as:
y t = sof tmax ( W hy ∗ h t + b y ) (1)

75ea7487-3d1d-4c83-b128-e1e7acce2302.label: text_chunk
75ea7487-3d1d-4c83-b128-e1e7acce2302.text:
where W hy are the transformation parameters, h t the hidden vector and b y the bias parameter. For a input sentence c [1: n ] with a label sequence l [1: n ] , a sentence-level score is then given as:
s ( c [1: n ] , l [1: n ] , θ ) = n X t =1 ( A l t − 1 l t + f Λ ( l t | c [1: n ] ))

ecef873f-c8d1-410c-8f2f-2fffa0886fac.label: text_chunk
ecef873f-c8d1-410c-8f2f-2fffa0886fac.text:
where f Λ ( l t | c [1: n ] ) indicates the probability of la- bel l t at position t by the network with parameters Λ , A indicates the matrix of transition probability. In our model, f Λ ( l t | c [1: n ] ) is computed as:
f Λ ( l t | c [1: n ] ) = − log ( y t [ l t ]) (2)

783ed171-102d-4d89-9c8c-1d3b0910b0ef.label: text_chunk
783ed171-102d-4d89-9c8c-1d3b0910b0ef.text:
We define a structured margin loss ∆( l, l ) as Pei et al. (2014):
∆( l, l ) = n X j =1 κ 1 { l j 6 = l j } (3)
where n is the length of setence x , κ is a discount parameter, l a given correct label sequence and l a predicted label sequence.

bba67ef7-a3f9-445e-9a79-6cf5afa86994.label: text_chunk
bba67ef7-a3f9-445e-9a79-6cf5afa86994.text:
For a given training instance ( x i , y i ) , our predicted label sequence is the label sequence with highest score:
l ∗ i = arg max l i ∈ Y ( x i ) s ( x i , l i , θ )
The label sequence with the highest score can be obtained by carrying out viterbi algorithm. The regularized objective function is as follows:

51afce5d-1aab-4237-ab45-813ad552ab2d.label: text_chunk
51afce5d-1aab-4237-ab45-813ad552ab2d.text:
J ( θ ) = 1 m m X i =1 q i ( θ ) + λ 2 || θ || 2 (4)
q i ( θ ) = max l i ∈ Y ( x i ) ( s ( x i , l i , θ )+∆( l i , l i )) − s ( x i , l i , θ )

11cf37e4-1a95-49e1-9df2-256af977a2e1.label: text_chunk
11cf37e4-1a95-49e1-9df2-256af977a2e1.text:
By minimizing the object, we can increase the score of correct label sequence l and decrease the score of incorrect label sequence l .

2.2 F-Score Driven Training Method

Max Margin training method use structured mar- gin loss ∆( l, l ) to describe the difference between the corrected label sequence l and predicted la- bel sequence l . In fact, the structured margin loss ∆( l, l ) reflect the loss in label accuracy.

87c2ebf0-b992-480c-8050-8b3109cc2a85.label: text_chunk
87c2ebf0-b992-480c-8050-8b3109cc2a85.text: Consider- ing the gap between label accuracy and F-Score in NER, we introduce a new training method to train directly on F-Score.

84443f1f-7838-42fc-b70b-2873b45096e9.label: text_chunk
84443f1f-7838-42fc-b70b-2873b45096e9.text:
To introduce F-Score driven training method, we need to take a look at the sub- gradient of equation (4):
∂J ∂θ = 1 m m X i =1 ( ∂s ( x, l max , θ ) ∂θ − ∂s ( x, l, θ ) ∂θ ) + λθ

b68fe338-5784-4813-8411-4afa6b352cc1.label: text_chunk
b68fe338-5784-4813-8411-4afa6b352cc1.text: In the subgradient, we can know that structured margin loss ∆( l, l ) contributes nothing to the subgradient of the regularized objective function J ( θ ) . The margin loss ∆( l, l ) serves as a trig- ger function to conduct the training process of B- LSTM based MMNN. We can introduce a new trigger function to guide the training process of neural network.

98aab5ee-2b12-4897-8b58-e5ea51f5b889.label: text_chunk
98aab5ee-2b12-4897-8b58-e5ea51f5b889.text:
F-Score Trigger Function The main criterion of NER task is F-score.

 However, high label accu- racy does not mean high F-score. For instance, if every named entity’s last character is labeledas O, the label accuracy can be quite high, but the precision, recall and F-score are 0 .

74d86040-4977-4300-bfcf-d6b743ee5daa.label: text_chunk
74d86040-4977-4300-bfcf-d6b743ee5daa.text:
We use the F- Score between corrected label sequence and pre- dicted label sequence as trigger function, which
can conduct the training process to optimize the F-Score of training examples. Our new structured margin loss can be described as:

198597a6-d49f-45fc-ba2c-39ef0d84fd50.label: text_chunk
198597a6-d49f-45fc-ba2c-39ef0d84fd50.text:
e ∆( l, l ) = κ ∗ F Score (5)
where F Score is the F-Score between corrected label sequence and predicted label sequence.F-Score and Label Accuracy Trigger Function The F-Score can be quite unstable in some situa- tion. For instance, if there is no named entity in a sentence, F-Score will be always 0 regardless of the predicted label sequence.

39e12788-9602-4969-9ae4-fecfc8be3e33.label: text_chunk
39e12788-9602-4969-9ae4-fecfc8be3e33.text:
To take advantage of meaningful information provided by label ac- curacy, we introduce an integrated trigger function as follows:
ˆ ∆( l, l ) = e ∆( l, l ) + β ∗ ∆( l, l ) (6)
where β is a factor to adjust the weight of label accuracy and F-Score.

7b2534de-16c7-4acb-82b3-e90b83af3aff.label: text_chunk
7b2534de-16c7-4acb-82b3-e90b83af3aff.text:
Because F-Score depends on the whole label se- quence, we use beam search to find k label se- quences with top sentece-level score s ( x, l, θ ) and then use trigger function to rerank the k label se- quences and select the best.

2.3 Word Segmentation Representation

Word segmentation takes an important part in Chinese text processing.

980fb607-c77f-444b-bab1-4b7b65d08595.label: text_chunk
980fb607-c77f-444b-bab1-4b7b65d08595.text:
Both Peng and Dredze (2015) and Peng and Dredze (2016) show the value of word segmentation to Chinese NER in social media. We present two methods to use word segmentation information in neural network model. Character and Position Embeddings To incor- porate word segmentation information, we at- tach every character with its positional tag.

 This method is to distinguish the same character at dif- ferent position in the word.

3c7fccaf-3f43-4dfb-b32e-e1358c7d3f24.label: text_chunk
3c7fccaf-3f43-4dfb-b32e-e1358c7d3f24.text:
We need to word seg- ment the text and learn positional character em- beddings from the segmented text.Character Embeddings and Word Segmenta- tion Features We can treat word segmentation as discrete features in neural network model. The discrete features can be easily incorporated into neural network model (Collobert et al., 2011).

 We use word embeddings from a LSTM pretrained on MSRA 2006 corpus to initialize the word segmen- tation features.

4 Conclusions and Future Work

The results of our experiments also suggest direc- tions for future work. We can observe all models in Table 3 achieve a much lower recall than pre- cision (Pink et al., 2014).

1. **Symbolic Approach and Tokenizer**: The symbolic approach relies on the tokenizer to segment text into tokens, which is essential for its methodology.

2. **Text Representation Model and Training**: The Text Representation Model utilizes the training process to optimize its parameters based on the data it processes.

3. **RANDOM and Whitespace**: The RANDOM algorithm manipulates whitespace by replacing it with the '@' character during segmentation, establishing a direct relationship between the algorithm and the character type.

4. **fastText and ngrams**: fastText employs ngrams in its text processing and embedding generation techniques, which are vital for understanding and representing language structure.

5. **9-grams and Delimiter**: 9-grams use delimiters to define boundaries within sequences, facilitating structured analysis of text data.

6. **Sequence Classification and Tokenization-based Approaches**: Sequence classification depends on tokenization-based approaches as a preprocessing step to effectively categorize character sequences.

7. **Models and Tokenization**: Various models utilize tokenization as a fundamental process to convert text into manageable units (tokens) for further analysis.

8. **Averaging Character Embeddings and Character Embeddings**: Averaging character embeddings is a technique that uses character embeddings to create a single averaged representation.

9. **Neural Machine Translation and Joint Attention/Translation Model**: Neural Machine Translation employs the Joint Attention/Translation Model to enhance alignment and translation processes, making it a crucial element in translation workflows.

This summary encapsulates the key entities and their interrelationships, highlighting the methodologies and processes involved in text processing and analysis.

1. **Tokenization and NLP**: Tokenization is described as a fundamental process within the NLP pipeline, serving as one of the initial steps for analyzing and understanding text data. It is crucial for breaking down text into manageable units for further analysis.

2. **NLP and Tokenization**: The relationship emphasizes that tokenization is a key process used in NLP, reinforcing its importance in preparing text for deeper analytical tasks.

Overall, the synthesis of these relationships underscores that tokenization is integral to NLP, facilitating the effective processing and comprehension of natural language data.

### Summary of Relationships

1. **Pointwise Mutual Information Matrix & Skipgram Model**: The Skipgram Model utilizes the Pointwise Mutual Information Matrix to analyze word relationships based on their co-occurrence in various contexts. This highlights the model's reliance on statistical measures to enhance its understanding of language.

2. **A-RANDOM & Wikipedia**: A-RANDOM employs contexts selected from Wikipedia to generate its embeddings. This indicates a significant connection between the algorithm and Wikipedia as a data source, showcasing how external knowledge bases can inform machine learning processes.

3. **Nonsymbolic Representation Learning & Tokenization-free Approaches**: Nonsymbolic representation learning leverages tokenization-free approaches to process data, moving beyond traditional tokenization methods. This relationship emphasizes a more holistic understanding of data, allowing for richer representations without the limitations imposed by conventional tokenization.

Overall, these relationships illustrate the interconnectedness of various models and approaches in natural language processing, emphasizing the importance of data sources and innovative techniques in enhancing machine learning capabilities.

1. **POS (Part-of-Speech Tagging)**:
   - **Relationship**: Used in analyzing the text of best-selling monthly comic book publications.
   - **Description**: POS tagging aids in understanding the context and structure of language within these publications, enhancing text analysis.

2. **K-grams**:
   - **Relationship**: Used in extracting features from text for embeddings.
   - **Description**: K-grams facilitate the extraction of textual features, which are then represented as embeddings for various NLP tasks.

3. **Embeddings**:
   - **Relationship**: Represented as a result of feature extraction from text using k-grams.
   - **Description**: These embeddings are crucial for performing various natural language processing tasks.

The relationships outlined in the knowledge graph highlight key connections between linguistic concepts and their applications in language processing:

1. **Ngram and Form-Meaning Homomorphism**: Ngrams are sequences of words that can be analyzed through the principle of form-meaning homomorphism, indicating that these sequences not only represent linguistic forms but also convey meaning, establishing a correspondence between structure and semantics.

2. **English Text and Disambiguation**: The clarity of well-edited English text relies on disambiguation, which is essential for ensuring that the meanings of words are clear and contextually appropriate, thereby enhancing comprehension and communication.

3. **Morphological Segmentation and Word Representation Models**: Morphological segmentation plays a crucial role in word representation models by providing a framework for breaking down words into smaller units. This decomposition allows for various representations of words, facilitating better understanding and processing in linguistic applications.

Overall, these relationships emphasize the interconnectedness of linguistic structures, the importance of clarity in language, and the methodologies used to represent and analyze language effectively.
